name: Hussain ibn Ali
title: Master of Martyrs
role: Imam and Leader of the Uprising
shortDescription: The third Shia Imam and grandson of the Prophet, who created an unparalleled epic to save religion from deviation.
fullStory:
Hussain ibn Ali (AS) is the son of Amir al-Mu'minin Ali (AS) and Fatima al-Zahra (SA), the beloved grandson of the Messenger of God (PBUH), and the third Imam of Shia Muslims. He was raised in the cradle of revelation and was always deeply respected and loved by the Prophet during his lifetime, to the extent that the Prophet famously said: 'Hussain is from me, and I am from Hussain'. His lofty position among Muslims as a symbol of piety, knowledge, and bravery was universally acknowledged.

After the death of Muawiyah, when Yazid unjustly seized the caliphate and ordered the governor of Medina to extract allegiance from Imam Hussain (AS), the Imam firmly rejected this demand, stating: 'A person like me does not pledge allegiance to a person like Yazid'. To preserve Islam from fundamental deviations and awaken the dormant conscience of the Islamic society, he left Medina and traveled to Mecca to find a safe haven for enlightenment.

During this time, the people of Kufa sent thousands of letters inviting him to come to Kufa and lead them against the tyranny of the Umayyads. Imam Hussain (AS) departed toward Kufa with his family and close companions. However, intimidated and bribed by the heavy-handed government of Ibn Ziyad, the Kufans broke their promises. They intercepted the Imam's caravan and forced them to stop in the barren desert of Karbala, leaving the Imam and his small group of 72 companions alone to face a massive army of thirty thousand men.

According to the precise report of the book Al-Irshad written by Sheikh Mufid, on the day of Ashura, after Imam Hussain (AS) lost all his loyal companions, brave brothers, and beloved children in the way of God, he entered the battlefield entirely alone. He requested a worn-out garment, tore it in several places, and wore it under his clothes so that after his martyrdom, the enemies would not strip his pure body out of greed for his clothes. The Imam fought with indescribable and astonishing bravery against the massive enemy army, and whenever he attacked their ranks, they scattered like a flock.

Sheikh Mufid continues his narrative in Al-Irshad, writing: Finally, Shimr ibn Dhi al-Jawshan and a group from the Kufan army attacked him. During this assault, Zur'ah ibn Sharik dealt a heavy blow to the Imam's left hand, and then Sinan ibn Anas al-Nakha'i ruthlessly thrust his spear into the Imam's body, bringing him to the ground. Khawali ibn Yazid attempted to sever his holy head but trembled and could not proceed, until Shimr (or Sinan) committed this horrific crime, and the Imam attained the supreme triumph of martyrdom. This magnificent stand remained the eternal symbol of seeking freedom and fighting oppression in the history of humanity.
quoteArabic: أَلا وَ إِنَّ الدَّعِیَّ ابْنَ الدَّعِیِّ قَدْ رَکَزَ بَیْنَ اثْنَتَیْنِ: بَیْنَ السِّلَّةِ وَ الذِّلَّةِ؛ وَ هَیْهاتَ مِنَّا الذِّلَّةُ
quote: Be aware that the illegitimate one (Ibn Ziyad) has forced me between two things: drawing the sword or submitting to humiliation; and it is impossible for us to accept humiliation.
